children.name: Apus
children.description: Masters of aerial life — swifts sleep, eat, drink, and even mate on the wing, landing only to nest. Common swifts arrive in Swedish towns in May and leave in July, spending the rest of the year airborne over Africa. Their screaming parties around rooftops on summer evenings are one of the defining sounds of Swedish cities.

children.name: Apus apus
children.description: Spends almost its entire life airborne, even sleeping on the wing at high altitude, and touches down only to breed in roof cavities. Its screaming summer-evening flights around town rooftops are among the most evocative sounds of the Swedish city.

children.name: Apus pallidus
children.description: A paler, browner relative of the Common Swift with a slightly broader-winged, more relaxed flight, breeding around the Mediterranean. A rare vagrant to Sweden, mainly recorded in autumn.

children.name: Apus nipalensis
children.description: A small swift of South and Southeast Asia that nests colonially under building eaves and rock overhangs. Has a conspicuous white throat and rump band.

children.name: Apus affinis
children.description: A small, square-tailed swift with a bold white rump, increasingly colonising southern Europe. Nests colonially under bridges and buildings.

children.name: Apus caffer
children.description: A slender, deeply forked swift with a narrow white rump crescent, breeding in old swallow nests. Has expanded into Spain and Morocco.

children.name: Apus horus
children.description: An African swift with a broad white rump that nests in burrows excavated by bee-eaters and martins. Named after the Egyptian sky god Horus.

children.name: Apus barbatus
children.description: A large dark swift of African mountains and cliffs, similar to the Common Swift but with a paler throat. Breeds on inaccessible rock faces.

children.name: Apus unicolor
children.description: A slim, uniformly dark swift endemic as a breeder to the Macaronesian islands of Madeira and the Canaries. Some birds winter in West Africa.

children.name: Apus acuticauda
children.description: A little-known swift of the eastern Himalayas with a needle-pointed tail, breeding on sheer cliffs. IUCN Vulnerable owing to a small, fragmented range.

children.name: Apus pacificus
children.description: A long-distance migratory swift breeding across eastern Asia and wintering to Australia, with a white rump band and deeply forked tail. A rare vagrant to Europe and North America.

children.name: Apus berliozi
children.description: A swift of the Horn of Africa and Socotra with a pale throat and rump, breeding on coastal cliffs and islands.

children.name: Apus alexandri
children.description: A swift endemic to the Cape Verde Islands, closely related to the Pallid Swift, with a pale throat and brownish plumage. Restricted to the volcanic archipelago where it breeds in rock crevices.

children.name: Apus balstoni
children.description: A swift found across Madagascar and the Comoro Islands, brownish-grey with a white rump, commonly seen foraging over open country and forest margins.

children.name: Apus batesi
children.description: A large, sooty-black swift of the Gulf of Guinea lowland rainforest with a square tail and very broad wings. Rare and seldom observed, breeding in large trees of West and Central African forest.

children.name: Apus bradfieldi
children.description: A pale swift of the arid escarpment regions of Namibia and western South Africa, closely resembling the Pallid Swift but with distinctly paler plumage. Breeds in cliff crevices in rocky gorges.

children.name: Apus classicus
children.description: A swift of eastern and southern Africa, sometimes treated as a subspecies of the Common Swift but now recognised as distinct by its more southerly breeding range and slightly different calls. Forages over a wide variety of habitats.

children.name: Apus excelsus
children.description: A medium-sized swift of east African highlands with a white rump and deeply forked tail, often forming mixed flocks with other swift species above montane forests.

children.name: Apus niansae
children.description: A swift of the East African highlands around Lake Victoria, with a dark sooty-brown plumage and shallow forked tail. Nests colonially in rock crevices on cliffs and escarpments.

children.name: Apus sladeniae
children.description: A dark swift found on Bioko (Fernando Po) and surrounding Gulf of Guinea areas, with a slightly paler throat and forked tail. An uncommon species of tropical forest highlands.

children.name: Apus toulsoni
children.description: A relatively poorly known swift of Angola's coastal lowlands with sooty-brown plumage, believed to breed on offshore rocky islets and cliffs. Closely allied to the Common Swift complex.

children.name: Tachymarptis
children.description: Large, powerful swifts with white bellies.

children.name: Tachymarptis melba
children.description: A large, powerful swift with a white belly and brown breast-band, breeding on cliffs and tall buildings in southern Europe. A scarce overshoot vagrant to Sweden, where its sheer size and slow wingbeats stand out.

children.name: Tachymarptis aequatorialis
children.description: A large African swift with a mottled grey underbody, soaring over highlands and gorges. Closely related to the Alpine Swift.

children.name: Chaetura
children.description: Spinetailed swifts of the Americas with stiff, spine-tipped tail feathers used to brace against vertical surfaces; many nest in hollow trees and chimneys.

children.name: Chaetura pelagica
children.description: A cigar-shaped North American swift that nests and roosts in chimneys, migrating to the western Amazon. IUCN Vulnerable due to steep population declines.

children.name: Chaetura vauxi
children.description: The smallest North American swift, nesting in hollow trees of western forests and famous for spectacular communal roosts in chimneys during migration.

children.name: Chaetura brachyura
children.description: A compact, pale-rumped swift of the Caribbean and northern South America with a very short tail, often seen low over towns and forest.

children.name: Chaetura cinereiventris
children.description: A small dark swift with a contrasting grey rump, ranging from Nicaragua through tropical South America over forest and clearings.

children.name: Chaetura spinicauda
children.description: A small spinetail swift of northern South America with a narrow pale rump band, foraging over forest and clearings.

children.name: Chaetura chapmani
children.description: A dark, glossy spinetail swift of tropical South America, nesting in hollow trees and chimneys.

children.name: Chaetura andrei
children.description: A spinetail swift of eastern South America from Trinidad south through Brazil, closely related to the Chimney Swift. It forages over tropical forest and is largely insectivorous.

children.name: Chaetura bourcierii
children.description: A mid-sized spinetail swift of Andean foothills from Colombia to Ecuador with a contrastingly pale rump, foraging over humid forest. Identification tricky where it overlaps with similar Chaetura species.

children.name: Chaetura egregia
children.description: A spinetail swift of western Amazonia and the Orinoco basin, distinguished from congeners by its notably pale underparts. It forages in mixed flocks above the forest canopy.

children.name: Chaetura fumosa
children.description: A uniformly dark sooty swift of Pacific-slope lowlands from Costa Rica to Colombia, with a nearly square tail. It hawks insects over forest and ocean coastline.

children.name: Chaetura josephinae
children.description: A large, pale-rumped Chaetura of Guyana and adjacent Amazonian Brazil, one of the larger spinetail swifts, nesting in the hollows of large rainforest trees.

children.name: Chaetura martinica
children.description: A swift endemic to the Lesser Antilles, found on Martinique, Guadeloupe and nearby islands, nesting in hollow palm trunks and tree cavities. One of the Caribbean's few breeding swifts.

children.name: Chaetura meridionalis
children.description: A migratory spinetail swift of southern South America that breeds in Brazil and Argentina and winters northward, hawking insects over open country and cerrado.

children.name: Chaetura ocypetes
children.description: A swift of northern South America from Venezuela to Trinidad, with a pale rump contrasting with its dark upperparts. Common over lowland forest and grassland.

children.name: Chaetura richmondi
children.description: A spinetail swift of Central America from Mexico to Panama with a dark, glossy plumage and pale rump, foraging in flocks above forest canopy.

children.name: Chaetura thomensis
children.description: A spinetail swift endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea, one of Africa's few Chaetura swifts. It nests in hollow trees of the island's montane rainforest.

children.name: Chaetura viridipennis
children.description: A spinetail swift of the central Amazon basin with a greenish gloss to the wing feathers, foraging over expanses of tropical forest. Closely related to Chaetura brachyura.

children.name: Cypseloides
children.description: Large, dark swifts of the Americas that nest in caves and behind waterfalls; among the most aerial of birds.

children.name: Cypseloides niger
children.description: A large dark swift of western North America that nests behind waterfalls and on sea cliffs, wintering in Amazonian Brazil. Notoriously hard to study.

children.name: Cypseloides cryptus
children.description: A poorly known dark swift of the Neotropics with a small white chin patch, nesting near waterfalls in mountainous terrain.

children.name: Cypseloides fumigatus
children.description: A medium-sized dark swift of southeastern South America that nests behind and beside waterfalls in forested highlands.

children.name: Cypseloides cherriei
children.description: A cliff swift with distinctive white spots on the lores, found from Costa Rica through Colombia and Venezuela. It nests on wet mossy rock faces behind or beside waterfalls.

children.name: Cypseloides lemosi
children.description: A very rare swift known from only a handful of records in Colombia and Ecuador, with a striking white chest patch on otherwise dark plumage. IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Cypseloides phelpsi
children.description: A dark cliff swift of the tepui highlands of Venezuela and adjacent Guyana, with subtle white markings on face and rump. It forages over sandstone tablelands.

children.name: Cypseloides rothschildi
children.description: A large, uniformly dark swift of Andean northwestern Argentina, nesting on wet cliffs and behind waterfalls in montane forest. One of the least-studied Neotropical swifts.

children.name: Cypseloides senex
children.description: The largest Cypseloides swift, a massive sooty-black bird that nests behind the famous Iguazu and other major waterfalls in southern Brazil, Argentina and Paraguay. Colonies may be large and dramatic.

children.name: Cypseloides storeri
children.description: A cliff swift endemic to the Sierra Madre del Sur of Mexico, with white forehead spotting, only described in 1993. It nests on cliff faces in remote mountains.

children.name: Streptoprocne
children.description: Large, powerful swifts of the Neotropics, often with white collars, that breed in caves and gorges and forage over huge areas.

children.name: Streptoprocne zonaris
children.description: A very large swift with a bold white collar, ranging from Mexico to Argentina. Forms large flocks foraging high over mountains and lowlands.

children.name: Streptoprocne rutila
children.description: A medium-large swift with a rufous collar in males, nesting in caves and behind waterfalls in the Neotropical highlands.

children.name: Streptoprocne biscutata
children.description: A large swift of eastern and central South America with two white patches at the neck sides, breeding colonially in caves and canyons.

children.name: Streptoprocne semicollaris
children.description: A very large swift endemic to the mountains of Mexico, with a white nape patch, breeding in deep caves and volcanic chasms.

children.name: Aerodramus
children.description: Echolocating swiftlets of the Indo-Pacific that nest in dark caves; the saliva nests of several species are harvested for bird's-nest soup.

children.name: Aerodramus fuciphagus
children.description: A cave-nesting swiftlet whose nest is built almost entirely of hardened saliva and is the prime ingredient of bird's-nest soup, supporting a major industry.

children.name: Aerodramus maximus
children.description: A swiftlet that builds its nest from saliva mixed with feathers, harvested as the lower-grade 'black nest'. Uses echolocation to navigate cave colonies.

children.name: Aerodramus spodiopygius
children.description: A small echolocating swiftlet of the southwest Pacific and northeastern Australia, nesting deep in caves in large colonies.

children.name: Aerodramus vanikorensis
children.description: A widespread cave-nesting swiftlet of island Southeast Asia and Melanesia that echolocates in darkness, with plain sooty plumage.

children.name: Aerodramus bartschi
children.description: An echolocating swiftlet endemic to the Mariana Islands, IUCN Endangered after declines from habitat loss and the invasive brown tree snake.

children.name: Aerodramus brevirostris
children.description: An echolocating swiftlet of Himalayan and Southeast Asian mountains, nesting in caves and gorges and descending to lowlands in winter.

children.name: Aerodramus salangana
children.description: A cave swiftlet of Borneo and nearby islands that builds nests of moss bound with saliva, echolocating in deep cave darkness.

children.name: Aerodramus amelis
children.description: A swiftlet of the southern Philippine islands, nesting in sea caves and limestone formations. Closely allied to the Edible-nest Swiftlet but producing a nest mixed with feathers.

children.name: Aerodramus ceramensis
children.description: A cave swiftlet endemic to Seram and surrounding islands of the Maluku archipelago, echolocating in darkness to locate nest ledges. Closely related to Aerodramus vanikorensis.

children.name: Aerodramus elaphrus
children.description: A small echolocating swiftlet endemic to the granitic Seychelles, nesting in caves and rock overhangs. The westernmost Aerodramus and one of the rarest swiftlets.

children.name: Aerodramus francicus
children.description: A swiftlet endemic to Mauritius and Réunion in the Mascarene Islands, nesting in sea caves and cliff crevices. Uses echolocation to navigate within dark nest chambers.

children.name: Aerodramus germani
children.description: A swiftlet of mainland Southeast Asia and nearby islands whose nests of saliva are commercially harvested in Vietnam and Cambodia. Closely related to Aerodramus fuciphagus.

children.name: Aerodramus hirundinaceus
children.description: A swiftlet of montane New Guinea, foraging over highland forest above 1500 m. It builds small saliva-and-moss nests in rock crevices and cave walls.

children.name: Aerodramus humphreysi
children.description: An echolocating swiftlet endemic to Chuuk and surrounding Caroline Islands in Micronesia. It nests in limestone sea caves and forages over the forest-covered volcanic interior.

children.name: Aerodramus inquietus
children.description: A swiftlet of Palau and the western Caroline Islands, foraging above coral-fringed islands and nesting in coastal sea caves. Uses click-echolocation to navigate in darkness.

children.name: Aerodramus leucophaeus
children.description: A swiftlet of Tahiti and the Society Islands in French Polynesia, nesting in sea caves and rock fissures. One of the most easterly distributed swiftlets in the Pacific.

children.name: Aerodramus luzonensis
children.description: A swiftlet confined to Luzon and nearby northern Philippine islands, nesting in sea caves and inland limestone formations. Its nest is a mix of saliva and plant fibres.

children.name: Aerodramus mariae
children.description: A swiftlet recently described from Maratua and nearby Derawan Islands off Borneo, nesting in sea caves along the island coast. Produces a commercially valued saliva nest.

children.name: Aerodramus mearnsi
children.description: A swiftlet of the southern Philippines, distinguished from other Philippine Aerodramus by subtle plumage and echolocation call differences. Nests colonially in coastal and inland caves.

children.name: Aerodramus mentawi
children.description: A cave swiftlet restricted to the Mentawi Islands off the west coast of Sumatra, breeding in sea caves and cliff crevices. Treated as a full species by IOC based on distinct echolocation calls.

children.name: Aerodramus nakamurai
children.description: A swiftlet of the Ryukyu Islands of southern Japan, nesting in sea caves and rock cavities. A relatively recently recognised species, separated from Aerodramus brevirostris by morphology and genetics.

children.name: Aerodramus nuditarsus
children.description: A swiftlet of the Papuan archipelago, characterised by featherless tarsi — unusual among swiftlets. It nests in sea caves and builds a compact saliva cup nest.

children.name: Aerodramus orientalis
children.description: A swiftlet of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, foraging over forest and coastline. Closely related to Aerodramus fuciphagus and produces a white saliva nest.

children.name: Aerodramus palawanensis
children.description: A swiftlet restricted to Palawan and nearby islets in the western Philippines, echolocating in limestone caves. Distinguished by subtle differences in nest structure and call from other Philippine swiftlets.

children.name: Aerodramus papuensis
children.description: A swiftlet of New Guinea's lowland forest margins and savannas, nesting in limestone caves. Part of a complex of similar Melanesian species sorted by echolocation calls.

children.name: Aerodramus pelewensis
children.description: An echolocating swiftlet endemic to the Palau Islands, nesting in limestone caves of the Rock Islands. A small, dark species with a pale rump that forages over the island's forested interior.

children.name: Aerodramus rogersi
children.description: A swiftlet of the Andaman Islands and nearby Cocos Islands, recently separated from Aerodramus salangana by morphological and genetic studies. Builds a nest of moss and plant fibres bound with saliva.

children.name: Aerodramus sawtelli
children.description: A swiftlet endemic to Atiu Island in the Cook Islands, one of the Pacific's rarest swiftlets, nesting exclusively in the island's makatea limestone caves. It relies entirely on these caves for nesting and echolocates within their darkness.

children.name: Aerodramus terraereginae
children.description: The only swiftlet breeding on the Australian continent, found in northeastern Queensland. It nests in caves and rock shelters, using echolocation and building a tiny cup nest of plant material and saliva.

children.name: Aerodramus unicolor
children.description: A uniformly dark swiftlet of peninsular India and Sri Lanka, nesting in highland caves and building half-cup nests of moss and saliva on cave walls. Common over tea estates and forest.

children.name: Aerodramus vanderbilti
children.description: A swiftlet of the Schouten and Biak Islands off the northern coast of New Guinea, one of several closely related Papuan cave-nesting species. Distinguished by voice and nest characteristics.

children.name: Aerodramus vulcanorum
children.description: A swiftlet of the volcanic highlands of Java, nesting in the crater caves and lava tunnels of active volcanoes. Known from only a few localities, it forages over upland forest and scrub.

children.name: Aerodramus whiteheadi
children.description: A swiftlet of the montane forests of Luzon in the northern Philippines, found at higher elevations than most Philippine swiftlets. Nests in mountain caves and deep ravines.

children.name: Collocalia
children.description: Small glossy swiftlets of Asia and the Pacific; unlike Aerodramus, most do not echolocate.

children.name: Collocalia esculenta
children.description: A tiny glossy blue-black swiftlet with a white belly, nesting in caves, buildings and culverts across the Indo-Pacific. Often seen in fast low flocks.

children.name: Collocalia linchi
children.description: A small dark swiftlet of Java, Bali and Sumatra with a greyish belly, building moss-and-saliva nests in caves and under eaves.

children.name: Collocalia troglodytes
children.description: The world's smallest swift, endemic to the Philippines, nesting in caves and crevices. Tiny and glossy with a faint pale rump.

children.name: Collocalia affinis
children.description: A small dark swiftlet of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands and nearby coasts, with a slightly notched tail. Nests in sea caves and coastal rock faces.

children.name: Collocalia dodgei
children.description: A swiftlet endemic to montane Borneo, known from the mountains of Sabah and Sarawak above 1000 m. Builds small saliva-bound nests on cave walls in highland terrain.

children.name: Collocalia marginata
children.description: A small glossy swiftlet endemic to the Philippines, with narrow white fringes on the secondary feathers giving a scaled effect. Nests in crevices and caves on forest-clad islands.

children.name: Hirundapus
children.description: Needletails — the fastest swifts in level flight, powerful large-bodied birds with spine-tipped tails.

children.name: Hirundapus caudacutus
children.description: A very large, powerful swift reputed to be among the fastest birds in flapping flight. Breeds in Asia and migrates to Australia; a celebrated vagrant in Europe.

children.name: Hirundapus giganteus
children.description: A large, fast needletail of forested South and Southeast Asia with a white throat and undertail, hawking insects high above the canopy.

children.name: Hirundapus cochinchinensis
children.description: A needletail of South and Southeast Asia with a distinctive silvery-white back and rump patch, hawking insects above forest and open ridges. A partial migrant within Southeast Asia.

children.name: Hirundapus celebensis
children.description: A large needletail of the Philippines and Sulawesi (Celebes) with a deep glossy blue-black and purple gloss to the plumage, and a white undertail patch. The largest needletail in its range.

children.name: Cypsiurus
children.description: Palm swifts — slender, long-tailed swifts that glue their nests to the underside of palm fronds.

children.name: Cypsiurus parvus
children.description: A slim, long forked-tailed swift of African palm savanna that builds a tiny nest of feathers and saliva on a vertical palm frond, gluing the eggs in place.

children.name: Cypsiurus balasiensis
children.description: A small, slender Asian swift closely tied to fan palms, attaching its pad-like nest to the underside of fronds. Fast and agile in flight.

children.name: Tachornis
children.description: Small Neotropical palm swifts associated with palms and feather-pad nests.

children.name: Tachornis squamata
children.description: A small, slender swift of Amazonian palm groves with a deeply forked tail, nesting in dead hanging palm leaves.

children.name: Tachornis phoenicobia
children.description: A small swift endemic to the Greater Antilles, nesting colonially in the dead skirts of palms. Abundant over Caribbean towns and countryside.

children.name: Panyptila
children.description: Swallow-tailed swifts of the Americas that build long, tubular hanging nests of plant down and saliva.

children.name: Panyptila sanctihieronymi
children.description: A large, deeply fork-tailed black-and-white swift of montane Central America that builds a remarkable long tubular nest hanging from cliffs.

children.name: Panyptila cayennensis
children.description: A small pied swift with a forked tail, ranging from southern Mexico to Brazil, that builds an elongated felt-like hanging nest tube.

children.name: Telacanthura
children.description: Large African spinetail swifts with white throats and rumps that nest in hollow trees.

children.name: Telacanthura usheri
children.description: An African spinetail swift with a white throat and rump band, nesting in hollow baobabs and other large trees. Spine-tipped tail aids clinging.

children.name: Raphidura
children.description: Small spinetail swifts with silvery-white rumps.

children.name: Raphidura sabini
children.description: A small, glossy black spinetail swift with a brilliant white rump, hawking insects above the canopy of West and Central African rainforest.

children.name: Rhaphidura
children.description: Small Asian spinetail swift with a bright white rump and needle-tipped tail feathers, related to African Raphidura.

children.name: Rhaphidura leucopygialis
children.description: A small, dark spinetail swift of lowland rainforest in the Malay Peninsula, Borneo and Sumatra with a gleaming white rump patch. Forages below and at the forest canopy edge, often with mixed swift flocks.

children.name: Mearnsia
children.description: Small Australasian spinetail swifts with boldly patterned black-and-white plumage, closely related to Zoonavena.

children.name: Mearnsia novaeguineae
children.description: A small spinetail swift of lowland New Guinea with a white rump and throat, nesting in hollow trees of gallery and lowland forest. Forages in fast low flights over rivers and forest clearings.

children.name: Mearnsia picina
children.description: A spinetail swift endemic to the Philippine island of Mindanao, with sooty-black plumage and a white rump band. Forages at forest edge and over lowland grassland.

children.name: Zoonavena
children.description: Small African and Malagasy spinetail swifts with white rumps, adapted to forest and open woodland.

children.name: Zoonavena grandidieri
children.description: A spinetail swift endemic to Madagascar with a white rump and throat, foraging over forest and open country. Nests in hollow trees and rock crevices.

children.name: Zoonavena marginata
children.description: A spinetail swift of Madagascar with white-edged inner wing feathers, foraging over forest margins and open farmland. Often seen in fast, erratic flocks over highland rice paddies.

children.name: Zoonavena thomensis
children.description: A spinetail swift endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea, with a white rump and dark upperparts. One of the island's endemic birds, foraging over primary forest.

children.name: Zoonavena sylvatica
children.description: A small spinetail swift of the Western Ghats of India, nesting in rock crevices and hollow trees of moist deciduous forest. Forages in fast, twittering flocks over forest canopy and ravines.

children.name: Neafrapus
children.description: Small, bat-like African spinetail swifts with very short, stumpy tails and broad wings, foraging in flickering flight over forest.

children.name: Neafrapus boehmi
children.description: A tiny, bat-like swift of East and Central African woodland with an almost tailless silhouette and rapid fluttering flight. Roosts and nests in hollow baobab trees.

children.name: Neafrapus cassini
children.description: A small bat-like swift of West and Central African lowland forest, with a tiny stub tail and white rump, foraging in flickering erratic flight at canopy level.

children.name: Schoutedenapus
children.description: African spinetail swifts of highland forest, closely related to Chaetura but restricted to the Afrotropics.

children.name: Schoutedenapus myoptilus
children.description: A medium-sized spinetail swift of east African highlands, superficially similar to Common Swift but with spine-tipped tail feathers. Found over montane forest from Ethiopia to Zimbabwe.

children.name: Schoutedenapus schoutedeni
children.description: One of Africa's rarest and least-known swifts, described from a single specimen collected in the Congo Basin. Known range restricted to the lowland rainforest of the Democratic Republic of Congo.

children.name: Hydrochous
children.description: A monotypic genus of waterfall swifts in Southeast Asia, specialised for nesting behind fast-flowing cascades.

children.name: Hydrochous gigas
children.description: A large, powerful swift of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra and Borneo that nests exclusively behind fast-flowing waterfalls in lowland rainforest. One of the largest swifts in the region, it builds a tiny nest of plant material glued to rock behind the water curtain.
